home.tagline: Mapping and celebrating the trees that grow on school campuses around the world.
home.map_hint: Hover over a dot to see school details. The map grows as new schools join.
home.mission_body: Schools Are Forests is a community initiative dedicated to documenting, mapping, and celebrating the trees that grow on school campuses worldwide. We believe every tree tells a story — of ecological history, community care, and the living connection between students and the natural world.
home.schools_subtitle: Explore the tree inventories of our partner schools.
home.what_maps_body: Every tree is precisely mapped so you can explore each school forest tree by tree from anywhere in the world.
home.what_photos_body: Each tree is photographed and catalogued with species, health status, and ecological notes.
home.what_data_body: Our inventories grow over time, tracking the evolution and health of each school's green spaces.
home.join_body: If you have a school tree inventory — or want to create one — we'd love to feature it on our map and help your community celebrate its trees. Schools from anywhere in the world are welcome.
home.join_map: Your school on the world map
home.support_subtitle: Schools Are Forests is a community-driven project. Your support — whether through volunteering, spreading the word, or a donation — helps us document more trees and reach more schools around the world.
home.spread_body: Share our project with schools and educators in your network.
home.volunteer_body: Help us with species identification, translations, fieldwork, or web development.
home.donate_body: Help fund field equipment, travel, and tool development.
home.ready_body: Dive into our interactive tree maps and discover the forests hiding in plain sight on school campuses around the world.

resources.hero_subtitle: Everything you need to measure trees accurately — illustrated guides, videos, and scientific references.
resources.measure_subtitle: Two key measurements every tree inventory needs: trunk diameter (DBH) and crown diameter.
resources.dbh_body: Wrap a measuring tape around the trunk at exactly 1.3 meters above the ground. If the tree grows on a slope, measure 1.3 m from the uphill side. Record the diameter in centimeters.
resources.crown_body: Find the widest point of the canopy and measure it end-to-end. Then measure again at a right angle. Record both values — North–South and East–West — in meters.
resources.citation: Illustrations adapted from: Roman et al. (2020). Urban tree monitoring: a field guide. USDA Forest Service, Gen. Tech. Rep. NRS-194.
resources.videos_subtitle: Short tutorials to help students and teachers get comfortable measuring trees in the field.
resources.videos_coming: Videos coming soon — check back here!

about.title: About Us
about.subtitle: We are a team of environmentalists, educators, and researchers passionate about urban forests and the communities that share them worldwide.
about.mission_body: Schools Are Forests is a community initiative dedicated to documenting, mapping, and celebrating the trees that grow on school campuses worldwide. We believe every tree tells a story — of ecological history, community care, and the living connections between students and the natural world.
about.vision_body: A future where every school community — no matter where in the world — knows, values, and tends to the trees around them. Where students grow up understanding the ecological importance of their school forests, and where schoolyards become living classrooms for the next generation of environmental stewards.
about.story_p1: Schools Are Forests began with a simple observation: school campuses are home to hundreds of trees — oak, maple, eucalyptus, jacaranda — that go largely unnoticed and uncelebrated. These trees provide shade, clean air, and habitat for birds and insects, yet most students and teachers couldn't name a single tree on their campus.
about.story_p2: We set out to change that, one school at a time, by creating detailed tree inventories that give each tree a name, a face, and a story. Our work has taken us across continents — and we're just getting started.

terms.title: Terms of Use
terms.subtitle: Please read and scroll to the bottom to continue
terms.scroll_hint: ↓ Scroll to the bottom to enable the button
terms.s1_body: Schools Are Forests is a global community initiative dedicated to documenting, mapping, and celebrating the trees that grow on school campuses around the world. By registering as a teacher, you agree to contribute tree inventory data to this open platform.
terms.s2_body: All tree inventory data submitted through this platform — including species, measurements, health status, GPS coordinates, and photographs — will be made publicly available. This data may be viewed by anyone on the Schools Are Forests website and through our public API.
terms.s3_body: By submitting data, you grant Schools Are Forests and authorized third parties the right to use, reproduce, and publish the data for scientific research, educational purposes, environmental monitoring, and related non-commercial activities. You will be credited as a contributing school where appropriate.
terms.s4_body: This platform does not collect personally identifiable information from students. Students access the platform using anonymous session codes. You are responsible for ensuring that no personal student data is submitted through tree notes or photographs.
terms.s5_body: You agree to submit accurate and honest measurements and observations to the best of your ability. Schools Are Forests includes a validation system to ensure data quality, and reserves the right to flag or remove data that is clearly inaccurate or inappropriate.
terms.s6_body: By uploading photographs, you confirm that you have the right to submit them and that they do not contain identifiable images of minors. Photographs will be publicly displayed alongside the tree data.
terms.s7_body: Schools Are Forests is provided "as is" without warranties of any kind. We are not responsible for any loss, damage, or harm arising from the use of this platform, including but not limited to inaccurate data, service interruptions, or unauthorized access to submitted data. Use of this platform is at your own risk.
terms.s8_body: You are responsible for maintaining the confidentiality of your account credentials and for all activities that occur under your account. Notify us immediately if you suspect unauthorized use of your account.
terms.s9_body: Schools Are Forests reserves the right to modify these terms at any time. Continued use of the platform after changes constitutes acceptance of the updated terms.
terms.s10_body: If you have questions about these terms, please contact us through the Schools Are Forests website.

tool.hero_subtitle: A browser-based field app for turning any school campus into a living tree inventory — no installation needed, works on any phone or tablet.
tool.how_subtitle: Three simple steps — from setup to a live tree map.
tool.step1_body: Create an account, add your school, define zones, and start a session. Students get a 6-character code.
tool.step2_body: Students go outside, enter the code, and follow the guided wizard: photos, species, height, trunk diameter — tree by tree.
tool.step3_body: Every tree appears on the school map the moment it's submitted. Teachers review and validate from the dashboard.
tool.learn_subtitle: A tree inventory is more than ecology — it's a hands-on lesson in several disciplines at once.
tool.learn_trig_body: Students use a protractor clinometer to measure the angle to the treetop, then apply trigonometry (tan) to calculate the real height — putting right triangles to work in the real world.
tool.learn_math_body: Measuring trunk circumference and converting it to diameter gives students a concrete reason to care about pi and unit conversions.
tool.learn_species_body: Students observe leaves, bark, shape, and fruit to identify the trees around them — building nature literacy on their own campus.
tool.learn_team_body: Each group divides roles — measurer, recorder, photographer, navigator — and has to coordinate to cover their zone efficiently.
tool.learn_science_body: Students collect standardized field data, learn why consistency matters, and see how individual measurements combine into a dataset.
tool.learn_env_body: Mapping the school's trees makes students think about urban green space, biodiversity, and their own campus as a living ecosystem.
tool.guide_subtitle: Everything you need to run a tree inventory session with your class.
tool.guide1_body: Go to Teacher Login and register with your email. After filling in your profile and school details, your account is reviewed by our team — you'll be approved within 24 hours.
tool.guide2_body: Zones are sections of your school grounds — think of them as areas you'll assign to different student groups. Each zone gets a letter (A, B, C…) and a location category (Playground, Garden, Front yard, etc.).
tool.guide2_tip: Tip: Create one zone per group of students. If you have 30 students in groups of 5, create 6 zones. Balance the number of trees per zone so the workload is even.
tool.guide3_body: From your dashboard, click Start Session. You'll get a 6-character code. Share it verbally, write it on the board, or print it. Students go to schoolsareforests.org/student and enter it. Sessions last 3 hours.
tool.guide4_body: Watch progress in real time from your dashboard. Once all trees are recorded, you can validate each submission — checking photo quality, species ID, and measurements. Validated trees are published to the public school map.
tool.need_subtitle: Simple materials — most schools already have them.
tool.need_tape_body: A standard tape measure works for trunk circumference and crown diameter. A diameter tape (reads diameter directly) is ideal but not required — the app converts automatically.
tool.need_clino_body: A simple homemade tool for measuring tree height — takes 5 minutes to build. Click to see step-by-step instructions.
tool.clino_items: 📐 Protractor (clear plastic preferred)|🥤 Drinking straw|🪡 String (~25–30 cm)|🪙 Small weight — nut, washer, or paperclip|🩹 Tape (masking or clear)
tool.clino_steps: Tape the straw securely along the flat, straight edge of the protractor (the 0°–180° line). This is your sight.|Thread the string through the small hole at the center of the protractor's straight edge.|Tie a knot on the back so the string won't pull through.|Tie the weight to the free end of the string. It must hang freely.|Test: hold it horizontal — the string should hang straight down and read 90°.
tool.clino_use_steps: Stand back from the tree. Measure your distance to the trunk (D) and your eye height (h).|Look through the straw at the very top of the tree.|Have a partner read the number where the string crosses the protractor — that is your angle of elevation (α).
tool.clino_formula_example: e.g. string reads 35°, D = 8 m, eye = 1.2 m → tree ≈ 6.8 m
tool.need_phone_body: Any browser works — Chrome, Safari, Firefox. No app to install. One device per group of 2–4 students is enough.
tool.need_sheet_body: No internet at your school? Students can record measurements on paper first, then enter the data later. Download the printable sheet below.
tool.print_body: Download and print the field data sheet before heading outside. Students fill it in by hand, and you enter the data later when back online. The sheet includes all measurement fields plus instructions for building the protractor clinometer.
tool.print_hint: Opens in a new tab — use browser Print → Save as PDF
tool.cta_body: Teachers set up the school. Students go outside. Trees end up on the map.

footer.tagline: Documenting and celebrating the trees growing on school campuses worldwide, one inventory at a time.

dashboard.how_1_body: Zones are sections of your school grounds — each assigned to a student group. Zone A might be the playground, Zone B the garden, etc. Each zone gets a letter and a location type. Divide them so every group has a similar number of trees to measure.
dashboard.how_2_title: 2. How many zones do I need?
dashboard.how_2_body: One zone per student group. If you have 30 students in groups of 5, create 6 zones. Balance the number of trees per zone so the workload is even.
dashboard.how_3_body: Once your zones are ready, go to the Zones tab and click Start Session. You'll get a 6-character code. Project it or write it on the board. Students go to schoolsareforests.org/student and enter it. Sessions last 3 hours.
dashboard.how_4_items: One phone or tablet per group — any browser, no app to install|A measuring tape — for trunk circumference and crown diameter|A cardboard triangle — for estimating tree height|Printed field sheets — if your school has limited internet
dashboard.how_5_body: After the session, go to the Validation tab. Each zone needs at least 1 review per 10 trees. Check photos, species ID, and measurements. Your school only becomes publicly visible once the minimum validations are complete.
dashboard.zones_hint: Create all your zones first, then start a session below.
dashboard.desc_placeholder: e.g. North side near the gate
dashboard.no_zones_hint: Click "+ Add Zone" to create the first zone for your students
dashboard.session_hint: Once your zones are ready, start a session to let students in
dashboard.student_url: Students go to schoolsareforests.org/student and enter this code
dashboard.publish_on: Your school is publicly visible on the map.
dashboard.publish_off: Your school is hidden — toggle to publish.
dashboard.publish_locked: Complete all zone validations to publish your school.
dashboard.no_zones_val_hint: Create zones in the Zones tab first
dashboard.location_title: How would you like to set your school location?
dashboard.location_subtitle: Choose one — either paste coordinates from Google Maps, or upload a KML boundary file from Google Earth (we'll calculate the center automatically).
dashboard.manual_hint: Use this tab to enter tree data collected on paper or by volunteers — one tree at a time. All data is saved directly to this school's inventory.
dashboard.delete_confirm: Delete Zone {{label}}? This will also delete all trees recorded in this zone. This cannot be undone.
